title: DAFW spends 99.9 paise of every rupee on revenue, not capital
dek: Object-head data shows the Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare allocates just ₹110 Cr to capital outlay in BE 2026-27 against ₹1.30 lakh Cr in revenue spending — almost the entire portfolio is transfers and subsidies.
body: When you drill DAFW to the object-head level, the picture is unambiguous: ₹1,30,452 Cr (99.92%) flows through the Revenue account — Farmers Income Support (PM-KISAN), Crop Insurance, Reserve Fund transfers, and grants to states under DAPSC/DAPST. Capital outlay sits at ₹110 Cr — less than 0.1% of the demand. This is not unusual for a transfer-heavy ministry, but it constrains the lever Government has to invest in long-lived agricultural infrastructure directly.

title: 24 new agriculture lines created in BE 2026-27, 14 discontinued
dek: Beneath the headline numbers, BE 2026-27 quietly opens 24 fresh budget lines in DAFW and shuts down 14 prior ones — a churn of 6% of the demand's object-head footprint.
body: Auto-flagged from the Detailed Demand for Grants: 24 sub-head/object-head combinations in Demand 1 are NEW (no prior allocation) and 14 are DISCONTINUED (had FY25 actuals or BE25 but no BE26). Most NEW lines cluster under 'Special Initiatives for development of Agriculture and Allied Sectors' — the largest YoY hike at +₹155 Cr (+62%). The discontinued lines are mostly small, but tracking them matters: the headline 'Agriculture got X% more' rarely reflects which programmes actually expanded.

title: Reserve Fund transfers are the single largest line in DAFW
dek: Transfers to Reserve Funds and Deposit Accounts absorb ₹72,400 Cr — more than PM-KISAN's Farmers Income Support — yet they're an accounting flow, not programme spending.
body: The largest minor head in DAFW is 'Transfer to Reserve Funds/Deposits Accounts' at ₹72,400 Cr (BE 2026-27), down ₹334 Cr from BE 25-26. These are book entries that move money to dedicated funds (notably the Agri Infra Development Fund built from cesses) — they show as expenditure but don't represent direct programme outlay. Stripping them out, the active programmatic spend in DAFW is closer to ₹58,000 Cr, of which Farmers Income Support (PM-KISAN) is ~₹50,000 Cr.

title: Three out of four rupees in MHA's police budget go to salaries
dek: Object-head data shows that across the central armed police forces — CRPF, BSF, ITBP, SSB — salaries dominate, leaving thin room for modernisation.
body: When you drill into the Demand for Police (No. 58) at the object-head level, salaries account for over 75% of revenue spending across the major central armed police forces. Capital outlay on modernisation — equipment, arms, surveillance — sits at roughly ₹4,290 Cr in FY26, less than the salary bill of the smallest of these forces. The pattern matters: when salary commitments are this rigid, marginal increases in the police budget translate poorly into operational capability.

title: Sagarmala absorbs the majority of capital spending at MoPSW
dek: The flagship maritime programme dwarfs every other capital line in the Ports ministry — and most of it routes through a single object head: Major Works.
body: MoPSW is small in budget terms (₹4,137 Cr in FY26), but the object-head view reveals a tightly concentrated portfolio. Sagarmala alone accounts for over ₹2,200 Cr of capital expenditure, and within that, the 'Major Works' object head carries ₹1,820 Cr. The Maritime Development Fund — newer, less scrutinised — adds another ₹1,272 Cr through the 'Investments' object head.

title: Mission Karmayogi is now DoPT's largest single outlay outside salaries
dek: The civil-services capacity-building programme has grown to ₹510 Cr in FY26 — bigger than UPSC's entire examinations budget.
body: DoPT is a small department, but the object-head data shows the centre of gravity has shifted. Mission Karmayogi, routed through the Capacity Building Commission, is now the largest non-salary line in the department at ₹510 Cr (FY26). For comparison, UPSC's examinations budget — covering every Civil Services Exam, NDA, CDS — sits at ₹392 Cr. The training institutes (LBSNAA, ISTM) together draw ₹363 Cr.
